tldr: Headlines split into two dominant angles: roughly half foreground Polgár's chess identity — 'chess legend', 'chess queen', 'Schachlegende' — treating the story as a celebrity nomination, while the other half lead with the PM's political action ('taps', 'gambit', 'ruling party backs'). The constitutional machinery removing Orbán-aligned president Sulyok, which created the vacancy, surfaces in only two headline slots and is effectively invisible to most readers.

evidence.quote: Hungarian PM's Presidential Gambit Fails as Polgar Declines
Hungarian PM taps chess grandmaster Judit Polgar to serve
Hungary's Prime Minister Magyar will ask chess champion
“NO!” PM Magyar Left Red-faced after Rejection
Hungary's ruling party backs chess legend Judit Polgar for
Легенда шахмат Юдит Полгар отказалась занять пост
Lenda do xadrez recusa convite para ser presidente da
Chess champion Polgar turns down offer to be Hungary's
Hungary's chess queen declines Péter Magyar's offer of role
Jewish Hungarian chess legend Judit Polgár turns down PM
Ungarns Präsident unterschreibt Gesetz für seine Absetzung
Hungary's Parliament Votes to Remove Orban-Appointed

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to use tabloid sensationalism in the headline — opening with a quoted “NO!” and describing Magyar as 'Red-faced', a tone entirely absent from every other bucket's title.
Only outlet to foreground Polgár's Jewish identity in the headline ('Jewish Hungarian chess legend'), a dimension invisible in every other outlet's title across the corpus.
Only headline exclusively about the constitutional process — Sulyok signing his own dismissal law — with no mention of Polgár; DW German covers the legal precondition, not the celebrity nomination.

bottom_line: The headline layer presents two parallel stories — a PM's political gambit that backfired, and a chess celebrity who declined a call to public life — while the constitutional removal of Sulyok that made the presidency vacant is nearly invisible at the front-page level.
